headline: Malaysia's Methane Emissions from Landfills Rise 28% Since 2018
context: Climate Watch data shows Malaysia's methane emissions from solid waste landfills rose 28% between 2018 and 2024, driven by increasing waste generation and insufficient sanitary landfill conversion.

cards.big: Methane is 80 times more potent than CO2 over 20 years.
cards.sub: Malaysia's landfill methane emissions rose 28% in 6 years — the fastest growth rate in ASEAN.

cards.big: Only 14 of Malaysia's 176 active landfills capture methane.
cards.sub: The remaining 162 vent methane directly into the atmosphere.

cards.big: Methane capture at landfills can generate electricity worth RM180M annually.
cards.sub: The Global Methane Pledge, which Malaysia signed in 2021, requires 30% reduction by 2030.

cards.big: Food waste comprises 44% of Malaysian landfill content.
cards.sub: Composting food waste would reduce methane while producing agricultural input worth RM320M.

cards.big: Malaysia signed the Global Methane Pledge in 2021. Emissions rose 28% since 2018. Pledges are not policies.
Methane from landfills is low-hanging fruit in climate policy — the technology exists, the economics work, and the climate benefit is immediate. The missing ingredient is implementation.
